story:
Houston returns to the park of his earliest memories with his dog, Retro—slides, swings, monkey bars, and the shade of old pines where he once threw a tennis ball to a puppy. He’s back in town to start his junior year at the university. Retro pads along, easy on the leash. A tennis ball bounces across the grass. A little girl with braided blond hair and striped overalls waves from a distance. She’s been tossing the ball their way over the last few days.

She runs up and says she knows him. “You’re Houston—Hosey—and this is Retro.” She mentions their first-grade teacher, Miss Menglekoch, the dreaded witch of the classroom. Houston gets a chill. How does this girl know all that? She claims she’s Annette, his first-grade friend. Impossible. He jokes about Neverland and Peter Pan, but she scoffs. She insists she’s Annette and produces proof—a worn Valentine card: “Dear Annette, my best bud, will you be my Valentin? Be mine, Houston.” He remembers the misspelling and the red-faced embarrassment. His stomach flips. She says she never grew up because she chose not to—she likes being a kid and not caring what other people think. “The time is now,” she declares in that announcer voice Annette used to use. Houston reels. He wants to leave but can’t.

The girl asks about growing older. He says there’s more freedom, more expectations. Then she hits him with a blunt question about sex. He sets a clear boundary: it’s for grown-ups, not a topic for kids, and not something he’s going to explain. Before it gets any stranger, a voice calls, “Danielle?” The girl turns. A young woman who looks like her is standing by the monkey bars.

She walks over and offers her hand. “Hi, I’m Annette. And you’re… Houston?” The world tilts into place. The girl is Danielle, Annette’s little sister. Danielle grins and says, “He told me about sex.” Houston denies it; Annette gives her sister a look, then sends her to play. Danielle zips away. Annette scratches Retro behind the ears; he licks her face as if she were an old friend.

The knot untangles. Danielle loves an audience and a stunt. She’s been rifling through attic boxes—old school stuff, old valentines. That’s how she had the card. Between the attic treasures and family stories, she picked up “Hosey,” Retro’s name, and the legend of Miss Menglekoch. She’s clever, precocious, and entirely too comfortable with fibs.

The shock softens into laughter. Houston explains he’s just moved back from upstate and doesn’t know anyone. Annette says she had to check when Danielle mentioned a “friend named Hosey” at the park. They trade updates. Both are starting junior year. Both are pre-med. No Miss Menglekoch, thankfully.

They decide to get coffee a few blocks away—just the two of them. Retro’s leash tightens, and he falls in beside them. Annette curtsies; Houston bows and reclaims his old nickname. They wave to Danielle. She waves back from the swing, hops down, does a little dance, calls to a friend, and climbs the monkey bars—content in the now. Houston and Annette walk on, old memories folding into new plans, childhood and adulthood holding hands for a few blocks more.
